bid.B_PASS_OPEN: With only {hcp} HCP you're below opening strength (about 12+). Pass.
bid.B_PASS_RESP: Partner opened, but {hcp} HCP opposite an opening bid isn't enough to look for game — so pass.
bid.B_PASS_ADVANCE: Partner has competed into the opponents' auction; with {hcp} HCP you've no fit to raise or suit to show, so pass.
bid.B_PASS_COMPETE: The opponents own this auction. With {hcp} HCP and no long suit worth an overcall (nor the shape for a takeout double), it's safer to pass than to act on nothing.
bid.B_PASS_SETTLE: You've already described this hand, and there's no fit or extra strength to chase. Pass and let the contract rest where partner can place it.
bid.B_PASS_NEUTRAL: This is a natural place to stop for your hand — pass rather than push the bidding any higher.
bid.B_1NT: {hcp} HCP and a balanced hand: 1NT pins your strength to a 15–17 range in one bid.
bid.B_1MAJ: {hcp} HCP with a {len}-card {suit} suit — open one of your five-card major so partner can find the fit.
bid.B_1MIN: {hcp} HCP but no five-card major, so open your better minor ({suit}) and await partner's response.
bid.B_2C: About {hcp} HCP — too strong for a one-bid, so open the artificial, game-forcing 2♣.
bid.B_WEAK2: A six-card {suit} suit but only {hcp} HCP — a weak two robs the opponents of bidding room.
bid.B_PRE3: A seven-card {suit} suit and only {hcp} HCP — preempt at the three level to crowd the auction.
bid.B_RAISE: {sup}-card support and about {pts} points — raise partner to {level} of {suit} to show the fit and your strength.
bid.B_1NT_RESP: {hcp} HCP with no fit and nothing to show at the one level — respond 1NT.
bid.B_3NT_RESP: {hcp} HCP, balanced with stoppers — bid the game, 3NT.
bid.B_NEWSUIT: Show your {len}-card {suit} suit: {hcp}+ HCP, and a new suit forces partner to bid again.
bid.B_STAYMAN: 2♣ is Stayman, asking partner whether they hold a four-card major.
bid.B_TRANSFER: A Jacoby transfer: it tells partner to bid {suit}, keeping the strong hand hidden as declarer.
bid.B_BLACKWOOD: Slam is in range — 4NT is Blackwood, asking partner how many aces they hold.
bid.B_DOUBLE_TO: A takeout double: opening values, shortness in {suit}, and support for the other suits — asking partner to choose.
bid.B_DOUBLE_PEN: Double for penalty — you expect to beat this contract.
bid.B_REDBL: Redouble — you're confident this contract is yours.
bid.B_REBID_NT: {hcp} HCP, balanced — rebid no-trump to pin down shape and range.
bid.B_REBID_SUIT: Rebid your {len}-card {suit} to show the extra length and let partner place the contract.
bid.B_OVERCALL: Overcall {suit}: a sound {len}-card suit and about {hcp} HCP — competing, and suggesting a lead.
bid.B_1NT_OVERCALL: {hcp} HCP, balanced, their suit stopped — overcall 1NT.
bid.B_GENERIC: {call} is the standard call with about {hcp} HCP and this shape.

play.ONLY: Only one legal card — you must follow suit.
play.LEAD_SEQ: Top of a sequence in {suit}: the {top} is a safe lead that promotes your lower cards into winners.
play.LEAD_4TH: Fourth-best from your longest suit ({suit}) — the classic opening lead to develop length.
play.LEAD_ACE: Against a suit contract you don't underlead an ace, so lead the ace of {suit} itself.
play.LEAD_LOW: Lead a low {suit} — a quiet, safe choice with nothing better to attack.
play.DECL_DRAW: You're declarer with the top trump: play the {card} to draw the defenders' trumps so they can't ruff your winners.
play.DECL_CASH: The {card} is the highest {suit} still out — cash your sure winner.
play.DECL_FINESSE: Lead low toward your {honor} of {suit}: if the missing higher honour sits before it, the {honor} scores. That's a finesse — a free shot at an extra trick.
play.DECL_RUFF: Lead your losing {suit} so the other hand can ruff it — turning a loser into a trick with a trump.
play.DECL_ESTABLISH: Lead low in your long {suit} to knock out the defenders' stoppers and set up later winners.
play.F_2ND_LOW: Second hand low: no need to spend an honour before seeing what partner and declarer do.
play.F_3RD_HIGH: Third hand high — the {card} is the cheapest card that can win, forcing out the defenders' higher cards.
play.F_WIN_CHEAP: Win the trick as cheaply as you can with the {card}, saving your higher cards.
play.F_DUCK_PARTNER: Partner is already winning the trick — play low and keep your strength.
play.F_CANT: You can't beat what's on the table, so play low and hold your honours.
play.RUFF: You're out of the suit led — ruff with a low trump to win the trick.
play.DISCARD: Can't follow and don't want to ruff — throw a low {suit}, your safest discard.

glossary.hcp: High-card points: Ace 4, King 3, Queen 2, Jack 1 — the main measure of strength. The deck holds 40, so an average hand has 10.
glossary.balanced: A hand with no void or singleton and at most one doubleton (4-3-3-3, 4-4-3-2, 5-3-3-2). Ideal for no-trump bids.
glossary.major: The spade and heart suits. Game needs only 10 tricks and scores well, so major fits are prized.
glossary.minor: The club and diamond suits. Game needs 11 tricks, so players often steer to no-trump instead.
glossary.no-trump: A contract with no trump suit; the highest card of the suit led simply wins each trick.
glossary.trump: The suit named by the contract. Any trump beats any card of the other three suits.
glossary.fit: A suit in which the partnership holds eight or more cards together — enough to choose it as trumps.
glossary.support: How many cards you hold in the suit partner bid; three or four is enough to raise.
glossary.raise: Bidding more of the suit partner already named, showing a fit and your strength.
glossary.sequence: Two or more touching honours (e.g. K-Q-J). Leading the top of one is safe and builds tricks.
glossary.fourth-best: Leading the fourth-highest card of your longest suit — a standard opening lead that develops length.
glossary.opening lead: The first card of the play, made by the defender to declarer's left before dummy is exposed.
glossary.finesse: Trying to win a trick with a card that isn't top by playing after an opponent, hoping a missing honour lies favourably.
glossary.ruff: Playing a trump on a suit you cannot follow, to win the trick.
glossary.draw trumps: Leading trumps repeatedly to strip them from the defenders so they can't ruff your winners.
glossary.declarer: The player who plays both their own hand and dummy, trying to make the contract.
glossary.dummy: Declarer's partner, whose hand is laid face-up and played by declarer.
glossary.defender: Either opponent of declarer; the defenders try to defeat the contract.
glossary.overcall: A bid made after an opponent opens — competing for the contract and suggesting a lead.
glossary.takeout double: A double asking partner to choose a suit, showing opening values and support for the unbid suits.
glossary.penalty double: A double made expecting to defeat the opponents' contract for extra points.
glossary.redouble: A call after an opponent's double that raises the stakes when you expect to make the contract.
glossary.stayman: A 2♣ response to 1NT asking opener whether they hold a four-card major.
glossary.transfer: A bid telling partner to bid the next suit up, so the stronger hand becomes hidden declarer (a Jacoby transfer).
glossary.blackwood: A 4NT bid asking partner how many aces they hold, used when exploring a slam.
glossary.weak two: An opening 2♦/2♥/2♠ showing a six-card suit and only 5–10 HCP, made to steal bidding space.
glossary.preempt: A high opening on a long, weak hand, made to crowd the opponents out of the auction.
glossary.stopper: A holding that halts a suit at no-trump (an Ace, or a guarded King, etc.).
glossary.vulnerable: Having already won a game this rubber; bonuses and penalties are larger when vulnerable.
glossary.game: A contract worth 100+ trick points (3NT, 4♥/4♠, 5♣/5♦). Two games win the rubber.
glossary.part-score: A contract below game; several can add up to a game across successive deals.
glossary.slam: A contract for 12 tricks (small slam) or all 13 (grand slam), earning a large bonus.
glossary.rubber: A match won by the first side to complete two games, worth a 500 or 700 bonus.
glossary.book: The first six tricks. A contract's level is how many tricks beyond book you must take.
glossary.honour: An Ace, King, Queen, Jack or Ten; a run of trump honours in one hand can earn a bonus.
glossary.second hand low: A defensive rule of thumb: playing second to a trick, usually play low and wait.
glossary.third hand high: A defensive rule of thumb: playing third to a trick, play high to help win it.
glossary.discard: Playing a card of another suit when you can't follow and choose not to ruff.
glossary.contract: The final bid: how many tricks the declaring side must win, and in which strain.
glossary.auction: The bidding, in which the four players compete to name the contract.
glossary.level: The number in a bid; add six to get the tricks required (4♠ = 6 + 4 = 10 tricks).
